column8: Cruz v. Fireflies.AI + Basich v. Microsoft + Illinois BIPA section 15
column15: Cruz v. Fireflies.AI and Basich v. Microsoft established that speaker diarization and vocal-pitch assessment constitute illegal biometric harvesting under BIPA. Standard in-call audio prompts do NOT satisfy BIPA §15 written-consent requirements. Statutory damages are $1,000-$5,000 per violation, per speaker, per session.
column16: your product does speaker diarization or voiceprint analysis on audio from BIPA-state users without BIPA §15 written consent — and Cruz v. Fireflies.AI plus Basich v. Microsoft just confirmed that "this call is being recorded" prompts don't satisfy the consent requirement
column17: $1,000-$5,000 per violation, per speaker, per session. statutory damages with attorney fees. and class certification for BIPA cases is clean — every user in the state is in the class by default
column18: DOC_AUP §3.6 — pre-built BIPA §15-compliant speaker-consent module.
column19: CONDITION_1: Product records OR analyzes OR processes voice/audio for speaker separation OR speaker identification OR voiceprint OR vocal-pattern analysis. | CONDITION_2: Product captures participants from Illinois OR US biometric states OR India OR EU/UK OR does not exclude those markets. | CONDITION_3: Product lacks one or more controls: written biometric consent OR participant notice OR retention schedule OR deletion route OR biometric policy OR call-level consent workflow. | CONDITION_4: Audio includes multiple speakers OR meeting participants OR customer/user conversations. | TRIGGER_IF: CONDITION_1 = TRUE AND CONDITION_3 = TRUE AND AT_LEAST_1_OF(CONDITION_2, CONDITION_4) = TRUE | EXCLUDE_IF: Product obtains written/recorded biometric consent, provides retention/deletion policy, and disables speaker biometric processing where consent is absent.

column8: Illinois BIPA (740 ILCS 14) + Texas CUBI (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code section 503.001)
column15: Illinois BIPA imposes strict liability of $1,000 per negligent violation and $5,000 per intentional violation. The Illinois Supreme Court has held that no actual harm is required; the statutory violation itself grounds the claim.
column16: your product captures biometric data from Illinois or Texas users without prior written consent and the statutory disclosures — which under BIPA and CUBI is strict liability: the violation itself is the injury, no actual harm required
column17: $1,000 per negligent violation, $5,000 per intentional, per user, per instance. class certification is near-automatic because every user is similarly situated. and the damages don't require proving any user was actually harmed

column8: Specht v. Netscape Communications Corp. (2d Cir. 2002)
column15: Specht v. Netscape established that mere availability of terms through a hyperlink without affirmative user assent fails to form a binding contract, voiding arbitration clauses, forum selection, liability caps, and class action waivers against that user.
column16: your signup uses a passive "by using this site you agree" banner instead of an unchecked checkbox — and under Specht, the contract with that user never actually forms
column17: the arbitration clause and liability cap in your ToS stop binding the users who signed up through that flow. the enterprise deal gets redlined. and any class action you thought was waived is back on the table.
column18: DOC_TOS §1.1 — pre-built Specht-proof §1.1 acceptance-flow module. drops in review-ready.
column19: CONDITION_1: Public terms OR onboarding uses one or more passive assent signals: continued-use acceptance OR browse-to-bind OR footer-only terms OR download/use-as-acceptance OR by-using-this-site language. | CONDITION_2: User can access OR sign up OR submit OR download OR buy without a separate affirmative assent act tied to the terms. | CONDITION_3: Terms link is footer-only OR hidden OR visually remote OR below the CTA OR absent at commitment point. | CONDITION_4: Product serves India OR US OR EU/UK OR global users OR does not exclude those markets. | TRIGGER_IF: CONDITION_1 = TRUE AND CONDITION_2 = TRUE AND CONDITION_3 = TRUE | EXCLUDE_IF: First-party signup flow OR checkout OR order form OR customer agreement OR UI shows affirmative clickwrap or signed assent before access/purchase, with conspicuous linked terms at the commitment point and evidence sufficient to defeat passive-assent conditions.

column6: Indian Contract Act, 1872 §§10, 13 + IT Act, 2000 §10A
column8: Meyer v. Uber Technologies, Inc. (2d Cir. 2017)
column15: Meyer v. Uber set the "reasonably conspicuous notice and unambiguous manifestation of assent" standard for mobile consent flows. Courts strike consent flows that fail this test, voiding the arbitration clause and liability caps for affected users.
column16: your mobile signup buries the ToS checkbox below the fold or stacks it with three other agreements, which fails the visual bar Meyer v. Uber set for mobile consent
column17: courts strike the arbitration clause for the mobile users. enterprise buyers flag it in diligence. and your mobile-first user base — the biggest group you have — is the easiest one to turn into a class action.
column18: DOC_TOS §1.1 — pre-built Meyer-calibrated mobile consent module. passes the visual-inspection test the first time.
column19: CONDITION_1: Mobile signup OR mobile checkout asks user to accept terms OR bundled agreements. | CONDITION_2: Consent has one or more defects: below-fold text OR tiny text OR hidden link OR separated checkbox OR bundled consents OR pre-check OR legal text not visible before tap. | CONDITION_3: User can proceed without separate affirmative assent at the action point. | CONDITION_4: Flow serves India OR US OR EU/UK OR global users OR does not exclude those markets. | TRIGGER_IF: CONDITION_1 = TRUE AND CONDITION_2 = TRUE AND CONDITION_3 = TRUE | EXCLUDE_IF: First-party mobile UI OR signup flow OR checkout documentation shows a conspicuous terms link and separate affirmative assent next to the CTA before completion, with consent not bundled or pre-checked.

column6: Indian Contract Act, 1872 §28 + Consumer Protection Act, 2019
column8: Bragg v. Linden Research, Inc. (E.D. Pa. 2007)
column15: Bragg v. Linden Research established that forcing users into distant, high-cost arbitration venues can be found unconscionable and unenforceable, collapsing the company's ability to compel arbitration and reopening the door to class litigation.
column16: your arbitration clause makes consumers travel across the country to dispute anything, with no small-claims carve-out and no cost-shifting — which is exactly what Bragg v. Linden called unconscionable
column17: you lose the ability to force arbitration. the class actions you thought were waived come back alive. and the clause enterprise buyers rely on when they buy from you gets struck in the same breath.
column18: DOC_TOS §14.3 — pre-built Bragg-proof venue module with the carve-outs courts actually honor.
column19: CONDITION_1: Dispute clause requires consumers OR small customers to use fixed distant venue OR foreign court OR out-of-state forum OR high-cost arbitration. | CONDITION_2: Clause lacks one or more controls: small-claims carve-out OR consumer forum OR user-location fallback OR online dispute option OR provider-paid fees OR statutory-remedy preservation. | CONDITION_3: Product is consumer-facing OR self-serve OR low-ticket OR sold to users unlikely to travel or pay for venue. | CONDITION_4: Product serves India OR US OR EU/UK OR global users OR does not exclude those markets. | TRIGGER_IF: CONDITION_1 = TRUE AND CONDITION_2 = TRUE AND CONDITION_3 = TRUE | EXCLUDE_IF: First-party terms OR dispute clause OR customer agreement shows local/user-accessible forum, statutory-remedy preservation, small-claims carve-out, online process, or provider-paid dispute costs sufficient to defeat unconscionable-venue conditions.

column8: ROSCA, 15 U.S.C. §8401 et seq. + FTC Act §5
column15: ROSCA and FTC Act Section 5 impose uncapped civil penalties and consumer redress obligations on "negative option" subscription flows where cancellation is materially harder than signup. Recent FTC actions have settled in the eight- and nine-figure range and named individual officers.
column16: your cancel flow takes more clicks than your signup flow — which is the exact pattern the FTC calls a negative-option dark pattern under ROSCA
column17: eight-figure civil penalties. consumer redress going back to every subscriber who ever tried to cancel. no cap on how far back they reach. and the consent decree changes how you run the business for years afterward.
column18: DOC_TOS §1.1 — pre-built ROSCA-sized cancel-flow module. keeps your name off the FTC complaint.
column19: CONDITION_1: Product has subscription OR free trial OR recurring billing OR auto-renewal OR negative-option plan OR credit renewal OR paid account continuing unless cancelled. | CONDITION_2: Signup OR renewal is online OR in-app OR self-serve OR otherwise low-friction. | CONDITION_3: Cancellation shows one or more friction signals: extra steps OR phone/email contact OR live chat OR hidden settings OR retention screens OR confirmshaming OR forced survey OR confusing buttons OR delay OR unclear effective date. | CONDITION_4: Product serves India OR US OR EU/UK OR global consumers OR does not exclude those markets. | TRIGGER_IF: CONDITION_1 = TRUE AND CONDITION_2 = TRUE AND CONDITION_3 = TRUE | EXCLUDE_IF: First-party billing UI OR account settings OR help documentation OR terms show cancellation is same-medium, clear, timely, self-service, and no more burdensome than signup.

column8: NY General Business Law §527-a
column15: New York auto-renewal law requires separate affirmative consent, or advance notice with cancellation rights where applicable, before certain subscription price increases are charged to consumers. Non-compliant renewal or price-change practices can trigger statutory claims, restitution, and class-action pressure for affected subscribers.
column16: your ToS lets you raise subscription prices on New York customers without a separate consent step — which is what the amended NY auto-renewal law started requiring in December 2025
column17: statutory damages for every affected customer. restitution going back to every hike that didn't get consent. and the class-action bar in New York has the list of every auto-renewal service that didn't update its flow.
column18: DOC_TOS §1.1 — pre-built NY auto-renewal consent module. keeps price hikes from becoming subscriber claims.
column19: CONDITION_1: Product offers auto-renewing subscription OR recurring paid plan OR membership OR paid account with future renewal charges. | CONDITION_2: Terms OR pricing materials allow post-enrollment price changes: price hike OR fee hike OR usage-charge hike OR plan re-tiering OR automatic migration. | CONDITION_3: Public terms OR checkout flow lack one or more price-change controls: separate affirmative consent OR advance notice with cancellation right OR renewal-specific acceptance OR pre-charge opt-out. | CONDITION_4: Product serves New York OR US consumers OR does not exclude those markets. | TRIGGER_IF: CONDITION_1 = TRUE AND CONDITION_2 = TRUE AND CONDITION_3 = TRUE AND CONDITION_4 = TRUE | EXCLUDE_IF: First-party checkout flow OR account settings OR renewal notice OR terms show price hikes require separate consent or advance notice with no-penalty cancellation before charge.

column8: UETA §10(2)
column15: UETA §10(2) grants users a non-waivable right to avoid the effect of an electronic record resulting from their error where the electronic agent gave no reasonable opportunity to prevent or correct the error. The right applies to automated transaction systems where no meaningful correction path is provided.
column16: your product finalizes transactions through an agent, auto-approval, AI execution, or automated workflow with no confirmation step, grace period, or error-correction UI
column17: every wrong automated action turns into a reversal or chargeback, with processor fees or unwind costs attached. the volume scales one-to-one with how many automated transactions the product runs. the more you scale, the bigger the reversal book gets.
column18: UI Mandate — pre-built UETA §10(2) correction module. stops automated mistakes from becoming reversals.
column19: CONDITION_1: Product includes automated flow OR AI action OR electronic agent OR auto-approval OR one-click execution OR workflow automation OR checkout system finalizing user-impacting transactions or account changes. | CONDITION_2: User can make material error before finalization: prompt error OR quantity error OR recipient error OR configuration error OR account error OR payment error OR wrong-option error. | CONDITION_3: UI lacks one or more correction controls: confirmation screen OR review step OR correction path OR reversal window OR grace period OR error-detection prompt. | CONDITION_4: Product serves US users/customers OR does not exclude US transactions. | TRIGGER_IF: CONDITION_1 = TRUE AND CONDITION_2 = TRUE AND CONDITION_3 = TRUE AND CONDITION_4 = TRUE | EXCLUDE_IF: First-party UI OR product documentation OR terms show meaningful pre-final confirmation, review/correction path, reversal window, or error-correction mechanism before the user-impacting action becomes binding.

column8: Mobley v. Workday (N.D. Cal. 2024-2026)
column15: Mobley v. Workday rejected AI vendor immunity from employment discrimination liability, allowing suit under an "agency" theory treating the AI vendor as acting on behalf of the employer.
column16: your product drives consequential decisions about people — hiring, lending, healthcare — while the architecture relies on "human in the loop" that's really just clicking approve, and Mobley v. Workday rejected that defense as vendor immunity theater
column17: direct liability for discrimination damages alongside the employer customer. class action exposure when the decision pattern affects a protected group at scale. and the "we just provide the tool" defense that the category relied on has been rejected on the record.
column18: DOC_AGT §2.2 — pre-built Mobley-resilient decision-architecture and human-judgment module.
column19: CONDITION_1: Product supports OR scores OR ranks OR recommends consequential decisions about people. | CONDITION_2: Product relies on vendor tool posture OR customer-side HITL that is nominal OR approval-only OR not substantively documented. | CONDITION_3: Decision context includes employment OR hiring OR lending OR healthcare OR education OR insurance OR similar high-impact domain. | CONDITION_4: Terms OR workflow lack one or more controls: meaningful human judgment OR bias audit route OR decision log OR customer responsibility allocation OR appeal/escalation route. | TRIGGER_IF: CONDITION_1 = TRUE AND CONDITION_3 = TRUE AND AT_LEAST_1_OF(CONDITION_2, CONDITION_4) = TRUE | EXCLUDE_IF: Workflow documents meaningful human judgment, decision logs, bias review, and allocation of vendor/customer responsibilities.

column8: Class Action v. Eightfold AI (2026) / Fair Credit Reporting Act
column15: Class action filings against Eightfold AI and parallel cases assert that AI scoring products meeting the functional CRA definition must comply with FCRA. Non-compliance triggers statutory damages of $100-$1,000 per scored candidate.
column16: your product scores candidates or individuals using data assembled from multiple sources — which meets the functional FCRA definition of a Consumer Reporting Agency — but you don't comply with CRA obligations like notice, dispute rights, or user access to scores
column17: statutory damages of $100-$1,000 per scored candidate, multiplied by every individual the product evaluated. attorney fees on top. and the CRA-compliance retrofit reshapes the product's data flow.
column18: DOC_AUP §3.4(a) — pre-built FCRA-aware CRA-status module. qualifies you for compliance or demonstrably excludes you.
column19: CONDITION_1: Product scores OR ranks OR profiles candidates OR individuals using data from multiple sources. | CONDITION_2: Output is used for eligibility OR suitability OR screening OR evaluation in employment OR similar decision context. | CONDITION_3: Product lacks one or more CRA/FCRA controls: notice OR authorization OR access/dispute workflow OR adverse-action workflow OR exclusion from CRA status. | CONDITION_4: Product serves US employers OR US individuals OR does not exclude US markets. | TRIGGER_IF: CONDITION_1 = TRUE AND CONDITION_2 = TRUE AND AT_LEAST_1_OF(CONDITION_3, CONDITION_4) = TRUE | EXCLUDE_IF: Product is demonstrably outside CRA scope OR includes notice, authorization, access, dispute, and adverse-action workflows.

column17: every transaction the automated system ran is treated as if you signed it yourself. the volume compounds with every scale milestone. and the "we didn't authorize that specific action" argument is dead on arrival under the statute.

column15: GDPR Article 28 requires executed Data Processing Agreements with every sub-processor. Articles 17 (erasure) and 20 (portability) require downstream cooperation that DPAs operationalize. Absence exposes the company to GDPR penalties up to €20M or 4% of global turnover.
column16: you process EU data through sub-processors — cloud infrastructure, APIs, analytics, payment rails — without executed DPAs covering each link in the chain, which is a straight Article 28 violation
column17: GDPR fines up to €20M or 4% of global revenue. supervisory authority orders. and the remediation work — negotiating DPAs with every vendor in the stack — takes months even with cooperation.
